gate_type: no gate on trigger — but exploitation requires attacker control of Omnisend HTTPS API response (compromised API key admin-secret, MITM on TLS to api.omnisend.com, or Omnisend server compromise)
reasoning:
The vulnerable code path (Sync::identify_user_by_id -> Snippet::set_contact_cookie_id -> setcookie()) is triggered by standard WordPress hooks: user_register, wp_login, and profile_update. These hooks fire for any user performing those actions — there is no nonce, capability check, or authentication gate protecting the code path itself. However, the actual exploitation primitive (injecting 
 into the contactID cookie value) requires controlling what the Omnisend API returns in the contactID field of its JSON response. That API call goes to https://api.omnisend.com/v3 or /v5 over TLS. To weaponize this vulnerability an attacker must be in one of three privileged positions: (1) possess the Omnisend API key (stored in WordPress options, readable only by admins/server processes), (2) perform a successful MITM against the TLS connection to api.omnisend.com (requires CA compromise or network-level access), or (3) compromise Omnisend's own infrastructure. None of these are achievable by an unauthenticated or low-privilege WordPress user in a default install. Additionally, the injected cookie would affect only the HTTP response delivered to the user who triggered the login or registration — not arbitrary third-party sessions. Finally, PHP 7.4+ (the dominant PHP version on modern WordPress installs) silently rejects 
 in setcookie() values (throws ValueError), eliminating the injection primitive entirely on current runtimes. The combination of: (a) privileged-position prerequisite to control the API response, (b) self-affecting-only impact (cookie goes to the registering/logging-in user's own browser), and (c) PHP 7.4+ mitigates the primitive — reduces the finding from Medium to Low. It is a real code defect worth fixing (add sanitize_text_field or preg_replace stripping 
 before the setcookie call) but is not exploitable by an unauthenticated attacker in a default WordPress environment.
